meaning_en: Refusing to compromise or change one's views
example: The intransigent negotiators refused every proposal.
mnemonic: INTRANSIGENT — IN + TRANSIT + GENT; a GENT who won't TRANSITION = intransigent.

meaning_en: Fearless and adventurous; brave despite fear
example: The intrepid explorer crossed the desert alone.
mnemonic: INTREPID — IN + TREPID (fearful); NOT fearful = intrepid.

meaning_en: Belonging naturally; inherent; essential
example: Creativity is intrinsic to the human spirit.
mnemonic: INTRINSIC — INTRO (within) + NSIC; coming from WITHIN = intrinsic.

meaning_en: A state of affairs that appears contrary to expectations
example: The irony was that the firehouse burned down.
mnemonic: IRONY — IRON + Y; as hard as IRON but with a twist.

meaning_en: Special words used by a particular group
example: Legal jargon is often difficult for non-lawyers to understand.
mnemonic: JARGON — JAR + GON; a JAR full of words GONE beyond understanding.

meaning_en: Placing two contrasting things side by side for effect
example: The juxtaposition of wealth and poverty was stark.
mnemonic: JUXTAPOSITION — JUXT (next) + POSITION; placed NEXT to each other in POSITION.

meaning_en: Using very few words; brief and concise
meaning_hi: संक्षिप्त / कम शब्दों में बोलने वाला
example: His laconic reply was simply: 'No.'
mnemonic: LACONIC — LACON (Sparta); Spartans of Laconia were known for brief speech.

meaning_en: To feel or express sorrow; a passionate expression of grief
example: She lamented the loss of her childhood home.
mnemonic: LAMENT — LA + MENT; LAMENTing means mentally mourning a loss.

meaning_en: To become weak or fail to make progress; to suffer
example: The reforms languished in committee for years.
mnemonic: LANGUISH — LANGUID + ISH; becoming sluggishly LANGUID = languish.

meaning_en: Generous giving of money or gifts
example: The king was known for his largesse towards the poor.
mnemonic: LARGESSE — LARGE + SSE; LARGE generosity = largesse.

meaning_en: Present but not yet visible or active; hidden
example: A latent talent emerged after years of neglect.
mnemonic: LATENT — LATE + NT; talent that came LATE = latent potential.

meaning_en: Lack of energy and enthusiasm; sluggishness
example: Post-lunch lethargy affects many office workers.
mnemonic: LETHARGY — LETHE (river of forgetfulness in Greek myth) + GY; forgetting to act = lethargy.

meaning_en: Expressed clearly; easy to understand; mentally alert
example: Despite her illness, she remained lucid and articulate.
mnemonic: LUCID — LUCID = LUCE (light); clear as light = lucid.

meaning_en: A plot or scheme; the action of plotting
example: The king was unaware of the machinations against him.
mnemonic: MACHINATION — MACHINE + ATION; a MACHINE of scheming = machination.

meaning_en: Generous or forgiving, especially towards rivals
example: The winner was magnanimous in victory.
mnemonic: MAGNANIMOUS — MAGNA (great) + ANIMUS (spirit); great-spirited = magnanimous.

meaning_en: Having or showing a wish to do evil
example: The villain had a malevolent smile.
mnemonic: MALEVOLENT — MALE (bad) + VOLENT (wish); wishing BAD things = malevolent.

meaning_en: An independent-minded person who does not follow the group
example: He was a maverick who challenged every convention.
mnemonic: MAVERICK — MAVE + RICK; RICK who MOVES his own way = maverick.

meaning_en: A deep, pensive sadness; a feeling of thoughtful sorrow
example: Autumn filled him with a quiet melancholy.
mnemonic: MELANCHOLY — MELAN (black) + CHOLY (bile); black bile causes sadness = melancholy.

meaning_en: Not telling the truth; lying
example: His mendacious account fooled no one.
mnemonic: MENDACIOUS — MEND + A + LIE; mending truth with a LIE = mendacious.

meaning_en: Very careful and precise about details
example: She was meticulous in documenting every finding.
mnemonic: METICULOUS — METI (fear) + CULOUS; so fearful of error = meticulous.

meaning_en: To make less severe or serious
example: Planting trees helps mitigate climate change.
mnemonic: MITIGATE — MITI (gentle) + GATE; opening a gentle GATE = mitigate.

meaning_en: A countless or extremely great number of things
example: The city offers a myriad of opportunities.
mnemonic: MYRIAD — MYR (ten thousand in Greek) + IAD; ten thousand = myriad.

meaning_en: The lowest point; the worst moment
example: The country reached the nadir of its crisis in 2008.
mnemonic: NADIR — NAD (nothing) + IR; the point where there is NOTHING left = nadir.

meaning_en: In the form of a cloud; unclear or vague
example: His plans for the future were nebulous at best.
mnemonic: NEBULOUS — NEBULA (cloud in space) + OUS; cloudy and vague = nebulous.

meaning_en: Wicked or criminal in nature
example: The nefarious scheme was uncovered by investigators.
mnemonic: NEFARIOUS — NE + FARIOUS (infamous); not famous for good = nefarious.

meaning_en: An opponent impossible to beat; just punishment
example: She finally faced her nemesis in the championship.
mnemonic: NEMESIS — NEMESIS (Greek goddess of revenge); inescapable retribution.

meaning_en: The rejection of all religious and moral principles
example: His nihilism left him without purpose or direction.
mnemonic: NIHILISM — NIHIL (nothing) + ISM; belief in NOTHING = nihilism.

meaning_en: A subtle difference in meaning or expression
example: Great writers understand the nuances of language.
mnemonic: NUANCE — NEW + ANCE; a NEW, subtle ANGLE of meaning = nuance.

meaning_en: To make unclear or confusing; to obscure the truth
example: Politicians often obfuscate rather than explain.
mnemonic: OBFUSCATE — OB + FUSCATE (dark); make DARK and confusing = obfuscate.

meaning_en: To destroy utterly; to wipe out completely
example: The bombing obliterated the entire village.
mnemonic: OBLITERATE — OB + LITERATE; wipe out (un-LITERATE) = obliterate.

meaning_en: No longer in use; outdated
example: Typewriters became obsolete with the rise of computers.
mnemonic: OBSOLETE — OB + SOLETE; SOLEly used in OLD times = obsolete.

meaning_en: Stubbornly refusing to change opinion
example: He was too obstinate to admit he was wrong.
mnemonic: OBSTINATE — OB + STINATE (standing); always STANDING firm = obstinate.

meaning_en: Giving a strong suggestion that something bad is going to happen
meaning_hi: अशुभ / बुरे की आशंका देने वाला
example: The dark clouds were an ominous sign.
mnemonic: OMINOUS — OMEN + OUS; full of bad OMENS = ominous.

meaning_en: Ostentatiously rich and luxurious; extremely wealthy
example: The palace was an opulent display of royal wealth.
mnemonic: OPULENT — OPU + LENT; so wealthy they LEND OPUlence = opulent.

meaning_en: To exclude from society or a group
example: She was ostracised after speaking out.
mnemonic: OSTRACISE — OSTR (oyster) + ACISE; shutting someone out like a closed OYSTER shell.

meaning_en: A typical example; a framework or model for understanding
example: The discovery created a paradigm shift in science.
mnemonic: PARADIGM — PARA (beside) + DIGM (show); a model shown beside all others.

meaning_en: A seemingly contradictory statement that may be true
example: It's a paradox: the more choices you have, the less satisfied you feel.
mnemonic: PARADOX — PARA (beyond) + DOX (belief); beyond normal BELIEF = paradox.

meaning_en: An outcast; a person rejected by society
example: After the scandal, he became a social pariah.
mnemonic: PARIAH — PAR + IAH; one who does not PAR (equal) with society = pariah.

meaning_en: The state of being equal in status or value
example: Gender parity in wages remains an unmet goal.
mnemonic: PARITY — PAIR + ITY; the state of being a PAIR (equal).

meaning_en: Overly concerned with minor details; showing off learning
example: His pedantic approach bored everyone in the meeting.
mnemonic: PEDANTIC — PEDANT + IC; a PEDANT obsessing over details = pedantic.

meaning_en: Having a harmful effect, especially in a subtle way
example: Social media can have a pernicious effect on mental health.
mnemonic: PERNICIOUS — PER + NICIOUS (hurt); through and through HURTING = pernicious.

meaning_en: Continued effort despite difficulties or obstacles
example: Her perseverance in the face of failure was inspiring.
mnemonic: PERSEVERANCE — PERSIST + EVER + ANCE; persisting FOREVER = perseverance.

meaning_en: Spreading widely throughout; present everywhere
example: A pervasive sense of hopelessness gripped the nation.
mnemonic: PERVASIVE — PER + VASIVE (spread); spreading PER (through) everything.

meaning_en: The highest point; the most successful point
example: She reached the pinnacle of her career at thirty.
mnemonic: PINNACLE — PIN + ACLE; a sharp PIN at the top = pinnacle.

meaning_en: A large or excessive amount of something
example: There is a plethora of evidence supporting the claim.
mnemonic: PLETHORA — PLETH (full) + ORA; full to OVERFLOWING = plethora.

meaning_en: Evoking a strong feeling of sadness or regret
example: The film ended with a poignant farewell scene.
mnemonic: POIGNANT — POIN + GANT; a POINT that CUTS to the heart = poignant.

meaning_en: A strong verbal or written attack on someone's beliefs
example: His book was a polemic against modern capitalism.
mnemonic: POLEMIC — POLE + MIC; grabbing the POLE and the MIC to argue loudly.

meaning_en: A practical approach rather than following fixed theories
example: Political pragmatism often means compromise.
mnemonic: PRAGMATISM — PRAGMA (deed) + TISM; acting on DEEDS not dreams = pragmatism.

meaning_en: Not secure or safe; dependent on chance
example: He lived in precarious conditions with no income.
mnemonic: PRECARIOUS — PRAY + IOUS; praying it doesn't fall = precarious.

meaning_en: An earlier event used as a guide or justification
example: The verdict set a precedent for future cases.
mnemonic: PRECEDENT — PRE + CEDENT; what went BEFORE (pre) = precedent.

meaning_en: A preference or special liking for something
example: She had a predilection for classical music.
mnemonic: PREDILECTION — PRE + DILECTION (love); a love that already existed = predilection.

meaning_en: Rapid increase in the number or amount of something
example: Nuclear proliferation is a major global concern.
mnemonic: PROLIFERATION — PROLIFIC + ATION; producing abundantly = proliferation.

meaning_en: Skill or expertise in a particular activity
example: Her athletic prowess earned her a full scholarship.
mnemonic: PROWESS — PRO + WESS; a PRO with WESSeling (wrestling) skills = prowess.

meaning_en: Acting with careful thought for the future; wise
example: It was prudent to save money before quitting the job.
mnemonic: PRUDENT — PRUDE + NT; a PRUDE is careful about everything = prudent.

meaning_en: A state of uncertainty; a difficult situation
example: She was in a quandary about whether to accept the offer.
mnemonic: QUANDARY — QUAN + DARE; daring (DARE) to QUEstion everything = quandary.

meaning_en: Bitterness or resentfulness
example: The political rivals spoke with great rancour.
mnemonic: RANCOUR — RANK + OUR; a RANK (foul) OURe of bitterness = rancour.

meaning_en: To formally approve and confirm a treaty or agreement
example: The senate voted to ratify the trade agreement.
mnemonic: RATIFY — RAT + IFY; make it RATE-official (ratify).

meaning_en: Having an obstinately uncooperative attitude
example: The recalcitrant student refused every suggestion.
mnemonic: RECALCITRANT — RE + CALCITRANT (kick); KICKING BACK = recalcitrant.

meaning_en: Morally correct behaviour; rightness
example: His reputation for rectitude made him a trusted judge.
mnemonic: RECTITUDE — RECT (right) + ITUDE; the state of being RIGHT = rectitude.

meaning_en: Saving or being saved from sin or error; recovery
example: He sought redemption for his past mistakes.
mnemonic: REDEMPTION — REDEEM + TION; the act of REDEEMing oneself.

meaning_en: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties
meaning_hi: लचीलापन / मुसीबत से उबरने की क्षमता
example: Her resilience after loss was truly remarkable.
mnemonic: RESILIENCE — REBOUND + SILENCE; bouncing back in SILENCE = resilience.

meaning_en: Admirably purposeful and determined
example: She remained resolute despite enormous pressure.
mnemonic: RESOLUTE — RESOLVE + UTE; a RESOLVED route that never bends = resolute.

meaning_en: A short period of rest or relief from something difficult
example: The ceasefire gave the population a much-needed respite.
mnemonic: RESPITE — REST + PITE; a moment to REST in spite of everything = respite.

meaning_en: Punishment that is considered to be morally right
example: The victims demanded retribution for the crimes.
mnemonic: RETRIBUTION — RETRI + BUTION; giving BACK (retri) what one deserves = retribution.

meaning_en: To think deeply about something; to meditate on
example: He sat ruminating on the failure for hours.
mnemonic: RUMINATE — RUMI + NATE; like RUMI the poet, thinking deeply = ruminate.

meaning_en: Too important or holy to be changed or criticised
example: In some families, Sunday dinner is sacrosanct.
mnemonic: SACROSANCT — SACRO (holy) + SANCT (sacred); doubly sacred = sacrosanct.

meaning_en: Having or showing keen mental discernment and good judgement
example: The sagacious elder guided the tribe through the crisis.
mnemonic: SAGACIOUS — SAGE + ACIOUS; wise as a SAGE = sagacious.

meaning_en: Making a show of being morally superior
example: His sanctimonious speech irritated everyone.
mnemonic: SANCTIMONIOUS — SANCTI + MONI + OUS; sanctifying MONEY = fake piety.

meaning_en: Grimly mocking or cynical
example: He gave a sardonic smile at the absurd suggestion.
mnemonic: SARDONIC — SARCASM + DONIC; sharp sarcasm with a donic (tonic) twist.

meaning_en: Severely critical; harshly denunciatory
example: The report contained a scathing review of government policy.
mnemonic: SCATHING — SCATHE (harm) + ING; harmfully critical = scathing.

meaning_en: Very concerned to act in a morally correct way
example: She was scrupulous in her research methods.
mnemonic: SCRUPULOUS — SCRUPLE + OUS; full of SCRUPLES (moral concerns).

meaning_en: Strongly influencing later developments; original and important
example: Darwin's work was seminal to modern biology.
mnemonic: SEMINAL — SEED + INAL; like a SEED that SPROUTS later influence = seminal.

meaning_en: Supreme power or authority, especially of a state
example: No country should violate another's sovereignty.
mnemonic: SOVEREIGNTY — SOVERE (sovereign) + IGNTY; the power of a SOVEREIGN.

meaning_en: Not being what it purports to be; false or fake
example: The magazine's claims were based on spurious research.
mnemonic: SPURIOUS — SPUR + IOUS; like a SPUR (fake urgency) = spurious.

meaning_en: Resoluteness and loyalty; firm in purpose
example: Her steadfastness in adversity inspired all around her.
mnemonic: STEADFASTNESS — STEADY + FAST + NESS; steady and FAST to purpose.

meaning_en: Very strict or severe, especially in rules or requirements
example: Stringent safety standards are mandatory in aviation.
mnemonic: STRINGENT — STRING + ENT; pulled as tight as a STRING = stringent.

meaning_en: So fine as to be difficult to analyse; making fine distinctions
example: The subtle shift in tone was barely noticeable.
mnemonic: SUBTLE — SUB + TLE; hidden BELOW (sub) the surface.

meaning_en: A person who acts to flatter influential people
example: The leader was surrounded by sycophants, not honest advisers.
mnemonic: SYCOPHANT — SYCO (fig) + PHANT; in ancient Greece, informers of fig-stealers = flatterers.

meaning_en: Understood without being directly stated
example: There was a tacit understanding between them.
mnemonic: TACIT — TAC + IT; a TACk left quietly, not nailed loudly = tacit.

meaning_en: Holding firmly to something; persistent
example: The tenacious lawyer pursued the case for years.
mnemonic: TENACIOUS — TENAC (hold) + IOUS; holding on tightly = tenacious.

meaning_en: A feeling of fear or anxiety about something uncertain
example: She approached the interview with great trepidation.
mnemonic: TREPIDATION — TREPID (fearful) + ATION; the state of being full of fear.

meaning_en: Making a loud, confused noise; very excited or emotional
example: The crowd gave the team a tumultuous welcome.
mnemonic: TUMULTUOUS — TUMULT + UOUS; full of TUMULT (disorder) = tumultuous.

meaning_en: A state of great disturbance or confusion
example: The country was in political turmoil after the coup.
mnemonic: TURMOIL — TURN + MOIL (toil); turning and toiling in confusion = turmoil.

meaning_en: Cruel and oppressive government or rule
example: The revolution was fought against decades of tyranny.
mnemonic: TYRANNY — TYRANT + NY; the city of TYRANTS = tyranny.

meaning_en: Present, appearing, or found everywhere
example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.
mnemonic: UBIQUITOUS — UBI (everywhere in Latin) + QUITOUS; everywhere = ubiquitous.

meaning_en: Never done or known before; novel
example: The pandemic caused unprecedented global disruption.
mnemonic: UNPRECEDENTED — UN + PRECEDENT + ED; no precedent exists = unprecedented.

meaning_en: Having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence
example: His vacuous comments added nothing to the debate.
mnemonic: VACUOUS — VACUUM + OUS; empty as a VACUUM = vacuous.

meaning_en: To regard with great respect and reverence
example: He was venerated as a saint in his hometown.
mnemonic: VENERATE — VENER (revere) + ATE; to hold in reverence = venerate.

meaning_en: Conformity to facts; truthfulness
example: Journalists must ensure the veracity of their sources.
mnemonic: VERACITY — VERA (truth) + CITY; a city of truth = veracity.

meaning_en: The action of keeping careful watch for danger
example: Security requires constant vigilance.
mnemonic: VIGILANCE — VIGIL + ANCE; keeping a VIGIL (watch) = vigilance.

meaning_en: Extremely unpleasant; morally bad
example: The conditions in the prison were vile.
mnemonic: VILE — VILE = FOUL; viley vile = disgusting.

meaning_en: Having a strong desire to hurt someone who has wronged you
example: She was vindictive and never forgave an insult.
mnemonic: VINDICTIVE — VINDICATE reversed; instead of clearing, attacking = vindictive.

meaning_en: Relating to deep inward feelings; instinctive
example: The horror film provoked a visceral reaction in viewers.
mnemonic: VISCERAL — VISCERA (organs) + L; coming from the ORGANS deep inside = visceral.

meaning_en: Full of bitter criticism or hatred
example: His vitriolic attack on the opposition shocked many.
mnemonic: VITRIOLIC — VITRIOL (corrosive acid) + IC; burning like acid = vitriolic.

meaning_en: Liable to change rapidly and unpredictably; explosive
example: The region's volatile political situation worried investors.
mnemonic: VOLATILE — VOLAT (fly) + ILE; flying away at any moment = volatile.

meaning_en: Extreme anger; divine punishment
example: The dictator feared the wrath of the people.
mnemonic: WRATH — BATH of anger; so angry it burns like a LATH (flame) = wrath.

meaning_en: Dislike or prejudice against people from other countries
example: Xenophobia rose sharply during the economic crisis.
mnemonic: XENOPHOBIA — XENO (foreign) + PHOBIA (fear); fear of FOREIGNERS = xenophobia.

meaning_en: A fanatical and uncompromising follower of a cause
example: Religious zealots protested outside the theatre.
mnemonic: ZEALOT — ZEAL + OT; one who has excessive ZEAL = zealot.

meaning_en: The highest point reached by the sun; the peak of achievement
example: The empire was at its zenith in the 17th century.
mnemonic: ZENITH — ZEN + ITH; a ZEN master at the ITH (highest) level = zenith.

meaning_en: The defining spirit or mood of a particular period in history
example: Social media is very much the zeitgeist of our era.
mnemonic: ZEITGEIST — ZEIT (time) + GEIST (spirit); the SPIRIT of the TIME = zeitgeist.
